title: Gene Ontology

infobox.value: Resource with [controlled vocabulary](/wiki/Controlled_vocabulary "Controlled vocabulary") to describe the function of [genes](/wiki/Gene "Gene") and [gene products](/wiki/Gene_product "Gene product")

(**GO**) is a major [bioinformatics](/wiki/Bioinformatics "Bioinformatics") initiative to unify the representation of [gene](/wiki/Gene "Gene") and [gene product](/wiki/Gene_product "Gene product") attributes across all [species](/wiki/Species "Species").[[1]](#cite_note-pmid17984083-1) More specifically, the project aims to: 1) maintain and develop its [controlled vocabulary](/wiki/Controlled_vocabulary "Controlled vocabulary") of gene and gene product attributes; 2) [annotate](/wiki/Genome_annotation "Genome annotation") genes and gene products, and assimilate and disseminate annotation data; and 3) provide tools for easy access to all aspects of the data provided by the project, and to enable functional interpretation of experimental data using the GO, for example via enrichment analysis.[[2]](#cite_note-GO-2)[[3]](#cite_note-GaudetŠkunca2017-3) GO is part of a larger classification effort, the [Open Biomedical Ontologies](/wiki/Open_Biomedical_Ontologies "Open Biomedical Ontologies"), being one of the Initial Candidate Members of the [OBO Foundry](/wiki/OBO_Foundry "OBO Foundry").[[4]](#cite_note-pmid17989687-4)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Bioinformatics initiative

The Gene Ontology

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Content | |
| Description | Resource with [controlled vocabulary](/wiki/Controlled_vocabulary "Controlled vocabulary") to describe the function of [genes](/wiki/Gene "Gene") and [gene products](/wiki/Gene_product "Gene product") |
| Contact | |
| Primary citation | [PMID](/wiki/PMID_(identifier) "PMID (identifier)") [36866529](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36866529) |
| Access | |
| Website | [geneontology.org](http://geneontology.org) |
| Miscellaneous | |
| [License](/wiki/Software_license "Software license") | CC BY 4.0 license |

The **Gene Ontology** (**GO**) is a major [bioinformatics](/wiki/Bioinformatics "Bioinformatics") initiative to unify the representation of [gene](/wiki/Gene "Gene") and [gene product](/wiki/Gene_product "Gene product") attributes across all [species](/wiki/Species "Species").[[1]](#cite_note-pmid17984083-1) More specifically, the project aims to: 1) maintain and develop its [controlled vocabulary](/wiki/Controlled_vocabulary "Controlled vocabulary") of gene and gene product attributes; 2) [annotate](/wiki/Genome_annotation "Genome annotation") genes and gene products, and assimilate and disseminate annotation data; and 3) provide tools for easy access to all aspects of the data provided by the project, and to enable functional interpretation of experimental data using the GO, for example via enrichment analysis.[[2]](#cite_note-GO-2)[[3]](#cite_note-GaudetŠkunca2017-3) GO is part of a larger classification effort, the [Open Biomedical Ontologies](/wiki/Open_Biomedical_Ontologies "Open Biomedical Ontologies"), being one of the Initial Candidate Members of the [OBO Foundry](/wiki/OBO_Foundry "OBO Foundry").[[4]](#cite_note-pmid17989687-4)

Whereas [gene nomenclature](/wiki/Gene_nomenclature "Gene nomenclature") focuses on gene and gene products, the Gene Ontology focuses on the function of the genes and gene products. The GO also extends the effort by using a [markup language](/wiki/Markup_language "Markup language") to make the data (not only of the genes and their products but also of curated attributes) [machine readable](/wiki/Machine-readable_data "Machine-readable data"), and to do so in a way that is unified across all species (whereas gene nomenclature conventions vary by biological [taxon](/wiki/Taxon "Taxon")).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gene_Ontology&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The Gene Ontology was originally constructed in 1998 by a consortium of researchers studying the [genomes](/wiki/Genome_sequencing "Genome sequencing") of three [model organisms](/wiki/Model_organism "Model organism"): *[Drosophila melanogaster](/wiki/Drosophila_melanogaster "Drosophila melanogaster")* (fruit fly), *[Mus musculus](/wiki/Mus_musculus "Mus musculus")* (mouse), and *[Saccharomyces cerevisiae](/wiki/Saccharomyces_cerevisiae "Saccharomyces cerevisiae")* (brewer's or baker's yeast).[[5]](#cite_note-pmid10802651-5) Many other [Model Organism Databases](/wiki/Model_Organism_Databases "Model Organism Databases") have joined the Gene Ontology Consortium, contributing not only to annotation data, but also to the development of ontologies and tools to view and apply the data. Many major plant, animal, and microorganism databases make a contribution towards this project.[[6]](#cite_note-urlGene_Ontology_Resource-6) As of July 2019, the GO contains 44,945 terms; there are 6,408,283 annotations to 4,467 different biological organisms.[[6]](#cite_note-urlGene_Ontology_Resource-6) There is a significant body of literature on the development and use of the GO, and it has become a standard tool in the [bioinformatics](/wiki/Bioinformatics "Bioinformatics") arsenal. Their objectives have three aspects: building gene ontology, assigning ontology to gene/gene products, and developing software and databases for the first two objects.

Several analyses of the Gene Ontology using formal, domain-independent properties of classes (the metaproperties) are also starting to appear. For instance, there is now an ontological analysis of biological ontologies.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Terms and ontology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gene_Ontology&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Terms and ontology")]

From a practical view, an ontology is a representation of something we know about. "Ontologies" consist of representations of things that are detectable or directly observable and the relationships between those things.
There is no universal standard terminology in biology and related domains, and term usage may be specific to a species, research area, or even a particular research group. This makes communication and sharing of data more difficult. The Gene Ontology project provides an [ontology](/wiki/Ontology_(computer_science) "Ontology (computer science)") of defined terms representing [gene product](/wiki/Gene_product "Gene product") properties. The ontology covers three domains:

* [cellular component](/wiki/Cellular_component "Cellular component"), the parts of a [cell](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") or its [extracellular](/wiki/Extracellular "Extracellular") environment;
* molecular function, the elemental activities of a gene product at the molecular level, such as binding or [catalysis](/wiki/Enzyme_catalysis "Enzyme catalysis");
* [biological process](/wiki/Biological_process "Biological process"), operations or sets of molecular events with a defined beginning and end, pertinent to the functioning of integrated living units: cells, [tissues](/wiki/Tissue_(biology) "Tissue (biology)"), [organs](/wiki/Organ_(anatomy) "Organ (anatomy)"), and [organisms](/wiki/Organism "Organism").

Each GO term within the ontology has a term name, which may be a word or string of words; a unique alphanumeric identifier; a definition with cited sources; and an ontology indicating the domain to which it belongs. Terms may also have synonyms, which are classed as being exactly equivalent to the term name, broader, narrower, or related; references to equivalent concepts in other databases; and comments on term meaning or usage. The GO is structured as a [directed acyclic graph](/wiki/Directed_acyclic_graph "Directed acyclic graph"), and each term has defined [relationships](/wiki/Relation_(mathematics) "Relation (mathematics)") to one or more other terms in the same domain, and sometimes to other domains. The GO vocabulary is designed to be species-neutral and includes terms applicable to [prokaryotes](/wiki/Prokaryote "Prokaryote") and [eukaryotes](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote"), [single](/wiki/Single_cell_organism "Single cell organism") and [multicellular organisms](/wiki/Multicellular_organism "Multicellular organism").

GO is not static, and additions, corrections, and alterations are suggested by and solicited from members of the research and annotation communities, as well as by those directly involved in the GO project.[[8]](#cite_note-Lovering2017-8) For example, an annotator may request a specific term to represent a metabolic pathway, or a section of the ontology may be revised with the help of community experts (e.g.[[9]](#cite_note-pmid17267433-9)). Suggested edits are reviewed by the ontology editors, and implemented where appropriate.

The GO and annotation files are freely available from the GO website in a number of formats or can be accessed online using the GO browser [AmiGO](/w/index.php?title=AmiGO&action=edit&redlink=1 "AmiGO (page does not exist)").[[6]](#cite_note-urlGene_Ontology_Resource-6) The Gene Ontology project also provides downloadable mappings of its terms to other classification systems.

### Example term

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gene_Ontology&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Example term")]

:   id: GO:0000016
:   name: lactase activity
:   ontology: molecular\_function
:   def: "Catalysis of the reaction: lactose + H2O=D-glucose + D-galactose." [EC:3.2.1.108]
:   synonym: "lactase-phlorizin hydrolase activity" BROAD [EC:3.2.1.108]
:   synonym: "lactose galactohydrolase activity" EXACT [EC:3.2.1.108]
:   xref: EC:3.2.1.108
:   xref: MetaCyc:LACTASE-RXN
:   xref: Reactome:20536
:   is\_a: GO:0004553 ! hydrolase activity, hydrolyzing O-glycosyl compounds

Data source:[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Annotation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gene_Ontology&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Annotation")]

[Genome annotation](/wiki/Genome_annotation "Genome annotation") encompasses the practice of capturing data about a gene product, and GO annotations use terms from the GO to do so. Annotations from GO curators are integrated and disseminated on the GO website, where they can be downloaded directly or viewed online using AmiGO.[[11]](#cite_note-AmiGO-11) In addition to the gene product identifier and the relevant GO term, GO annotations have at least the following data:
The *reference* used to make the annotation (e.g. a journal article);
An *evidence code* denoting the type of evidence upon which the annotation is based;
The date and the creator of the annotation

Supporting information, depending on the GO term and evidence used, and supplementary information, such as the conditions the function is observed under, may also be included in a GO annotation.

The evidence code comes from a [controlled vocabulary](/wiki/Controlled_vocabulary "Controlled vocabulary") of codes, the Evidence Code Ontology, covering both manual and automated annotation methods.[[12]](#cite_note-urlECO:_Evidence_Code_Ontology-12) For example, *Traceable Author Statement* (TAS) means a curator has read a published scientific paper and the metadata for that annotation bears a citation to that paper; *Inferred from Sequence Similarity* (ISS) means a human curator has reviewed the output from a sequence similarity search and verified that it is biologically meaningful. Annotations from automated processes (for example, remapping annotations created using another annotation vocabulary) are given the code *Inferred from Electronic Annotation* (IEA). In 2010, over 98% of all GO annotations were inferred computationally, not by curators, but as of July 2, 2019, only about 30% of all GO annotations were inferred computationally.[[13]](#cite_note-urThe_what,_where,_how_and_why_of_gene_ontology—a_primer_for_bioinformaticians_—_Brief_Bioinform-13)[[14]](#cite_note-gocons-14)
As these annotations are not checked by a human, the GO Consortium considers them to be marginally less reliable and they are commonly to a higher level, less detailed terms. Full annotation data sets can be downloaded from the GO website. To support the development of annotation, the GO Consortium provides workshops and mentors new groups of curators and developers.

Many [machine learning](/wiki/Machine_learning "Machine learning") algorithms have been designed and implemented to predict Gene Ontology annotations.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-CozzettoJones2017-16)

### Example annotation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gene_Ontology&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Example annotation")]

:   Gene product: Actin, alpha cardiac muscle 1, [UniProtKB:P68032](http://amigo.geneontology.org/amigo/gene_product/UniProtKB:P68032)
:   GO term: [heart contraction; GO:0060047](http://amigo.geneontology.org/amigo/term/GO:0060047) (biological process)
:   Evidence code: Inferred from Mutant Phenotype (IMP)
:   Reference: [PMID](/wiki/PMID_(identifier) "PMID (identifier)") [17611253](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17611253)
:   Assigned by: UniProtKB, June 6, 2008

Data source:[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Tools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gene_Ontology&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Tools")]

There are a large number of tools available, both online and for download, that use the data provided by the GO project.[[18]](#cite_note-pmid18480123-18) The vast majority of these come from third parties; the GO Consortium develops and supports two tools, AmiGO and OBO-Edit.

AmiGO[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[11]](#cite_note-AmiGO-11) is a web-based application that allows users to query, browse, and visualize ontologies and gene product annotation data. It also has a [BLAST](/wiki/BLAST_(biotechnology) "BLAST (biotechnology)") tool,[[20]](#cite_note-20) tools allowing analysis of larger data sets,[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) and an interface to query the GO database directly.[[23]](#cite_note-23) AmiGO can be used online at the GO website to access the data provided by the GO Consortium or downloaded and installed for local use on any database employing the GO database schema (e.g.[[24]](#cite_note-24)). It is free [open source software](/wiki/Open_source_software "Open source software") and is available as part of the go-dev software distribution.[[25]](#cite_note-sfdl-25)

OBO-Edit is an open source, platform-independent ontology editor developed and maintained by the Gene Ontology Consortium.[[26]](#cite_note-26) It is implemented in [Java](/wiki/Java_(programming_language) "Java (programming language)") and uses a [graph-oriented](/wiki/Graph_theory "Graph theory") approach to display and edit ontologies. OBO-Edit includes a comprehensive search and filter interface, with the option to render subsets of terms to make them visually distinct; the user interface can also be customized according to user preferences. OBO-Edit also has a [reasoner](/wiki/Semantic_reasoner "Semantic reasoner") that can infer links that have not been explicitly stated based on existing relationships and their properties. Although it was developed for biomedical ontologies, OBO-Edit can be used to view, search, and edit any ontology. It is freely available to download.[[25]](#cite_note-sfdl-25)

## Consortium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gene_Ontology&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Consortium")]

The Gene Ontology Consortium is the set of [biological databases](/wiki/Biological_database "Biological database") and research groups actively involved in the gene ontology project.[[14]](#cite_note-gocons-14) This includes a number of [model organism](/wiki/Model_organism "Model organism") databases and [multi-species protein databases](/wiki/Biological_database#Protein_sequence_databases "Biological database"), software development groups, and a dedicated editorial office.

["The Gene Ontology project in 2008"](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2238979). *Nucleic Acids Research*. **36** (Database issue): D440–4.
